в”Ӯ Gap(MH)  в”Ӯ   8   в”Ӯ  165   в”Ӯ -639   в”Ӯ  1031  в”Ӯ  230   в”Ӯ ...    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ  рҹҹў   в”Ӯ  рҹҹў    в”Ӯ  рҹ”ҙ    в”Ӯ  рҹҹў    в”Ӯ  рҹҹў    в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

### Gap Row Highlighting Rules

| Gap Value | Color | Icon | Meaning |
|-----------|-------|------|---------|
| Gap > 500 | Light Green | рҹҹў | Comfortable capacity |
| 0 < Gap вүӨ 500 | Yellow | рҹҹЎ | Near capacity |
| -500 вүӨ Gap < 0 | Orange | рҹҹ  | Slight overallocation |
| Gap < -500 | Red | рҹ”ҙ | Critical overallocation |

---

## 5. Interaction Design

### 5.1 Timeline Controls

```
[вҶҗ] [Jan 2026 в–ј] to [Dec 2027 в–ј] [вҶ’]  [1M] [3M] [6M] [1Y] [ALL]
```

- **Date pickers**: Start/end month selection
- **Quick ranges**: 1 month, 3 months, 6 months, 1 year, All (12 years)
- **Arrow navigation**: Shift view by selected range

### 5.2 Hover Interactions

When hovering over a data point:
```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ February 2026          в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ Availability: 3,556 MH в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Adjusted: 3,247 MH     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Predicted: 4,102 MH    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Gap: +309 MH           в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Utilization: 91.3%     в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

### 5.3 Click Actions

- **Click on data point**: Opens detail panel for that month
- **Click on Gap cell**: Opens adjustment wizard
- **Double-click**: Zoom to selected month Вұ 3 months

### 5.4 Comparison Mode Toggle

```
[Without Adjustment] [With Adjustment] [Side-by-Side]
```

- **Without Adjustment**: Shows predicted workload without leveling
- **With Adjustment**: Shows leveled workload (optimized)
- **Side-by-Side**: Shows both charts stacked vertically

---

## 6. Responsive Design

### Desktop (вүҘ1200px)
- Full chart with data table below
- All columns visible in table
- Side panel for filters

### Tablet (768px - 1199px)
- Chart takes full width
- Data table scrolls horizontally
- Filters in collapsible header

### Mobile (< 768px)
- Simplified chart (key metrics only)
- Data table replaced with card-based view
- Swipe to navigate months

---

## 7. Accessibility

### WCAG 2.1 AA Compliance

| Requirement | Implementation |
|-------------|----------------|
| Color contrast | All text вүҘ 4.5:1 ratio |
| Non-color indicators | Patterns + icons for status |
| Keyboard navigation | Tab through data points |
| Screen reader | ARIA labels for all elements |
| Focus indicators | Visible focus ring on interactive elements |

### Alternative Views

- **Table-only mode**: For screen reader users
- **High contrast mode**: Black/white with patterns
- **Data export**: CSV/Excel for external analysis

---

## 8. Data Model (TypeScript)

```typescript
// Chart data point for a single time period
interface ManHourDataPoint {
  period: {
    year: number;
    month: number;       // 1-12
    label: string;       // "Jan-26"
  };
  taskCount: number;
  predictedWorkload: number;    // Before adjustment
  adjustedWorkload: number;     // After leveling
  availableManHours: number;    // Capacity
  availableManpower: number;    // Headcount
  gap: number;                  // available - adjusted
  utilizationPercent: number;   // (adjusted / available) * 100
}

// Chart configuration
interface ManHourChartConfig {
  timeRange: {
    start: { year: number; month: number };
    end: { year: number; month: number };
  };
  comparisonMode: 'without_adjustment' | 'with_adjustment' | 'side_by_side';
  fleetFilter: 'ALL' | 'KINKI_SHARYO' | 'ALSTOM';
  showDataTable: boolean;
  showGapHighlighting: boolean;
}

// Chart state for UI
interface ManHourChartState {
  data: ManHourDataPoint[];
  config: ManHourChartConfig;
  hoveredPoint: ManHourDataPoint | null;
  selectedPoint: ManHourDataPoint | null;
  isLoading: boolean;
  error: string | null;
}

// Gap severity classification
type GapSeverity = 'comfortable' | 'near_capacity' | 'slight_over' | 'critical_over';

function classifyGap(gap: number): GapSeverity {
  if (gap > 500) return 'comfortable';
  if (gap > 0) return 'near_capacity';
  if (gap >= -500) return 'slight_over';
  return 'critical_over';
}
```

---

## 9. Component Architecture

```
ManHourProjectionWidget/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ ManHourChart.tsx           # Main chart (recharts/visx)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ ManHourDataTable.tsx       # Data table below chart
в”ңв”Җв”Җ TimelineControls.tsx       # Date range selector
в”ңв”Җв”Җ ComparisonToggle.tsx       # Without/With adjustment toggle
в”ңв”Җв”Җ ChartTooltip.tsx           # Hover tooltip
в”ңв”Җв”Җ GapIndicator.tsx           # Gap cell with color coding
в”ңв”Җв”Җ ManHourDetailPanel.tsx     # Side panel for month details
в””в”Җв”Җ hooks/
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ useManHourData.ts      # Data fetching hook
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ useChartConfig.ts      # Configuration state
    в””в”Җв”Җ useGapAnalysis.ts      # Gap calculations
```

---

## 10. Integration Points

### Data Sources
- `ResourceLevelerService.getProjection(timeRange)` - Get man-hour projections
- `SimulationService.compareScenarios()` - Compare adjustment strategies

### Events Emitted
- `onPeriodSelect(period)` - When user selects a specific month
- `onAdjustmentRequest(period)` - When user wants to adjust a month
- `onExport(format)` - When user exports chart/data

### Events Consumed
- `maintenanceScheduleUpdated` - Refresh chart data
- `resourceConfigChanged` - Recalculate availability

---

## 11. Performance Considerations

| Scenario | Data Points | Strategy |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| 1 Year | 12 | Render all |
| 5 Years | 60 | Render all |
| 12 Years | 144 | Virtualize table, aggregate chart |

### Optimization Strategies
1. **Lazy load data table rows** beyond viewport
2. **Aggregate to quarters** for views > 3 years
3. **Debounce hover events** (100ms)
4. **Memoize gap calculations**

---

## 12. Unknown Unknowns Discovered

Through competitor research, several risks identified:

| Risk | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|--------|------------|
| **Timezone issues** | Man-hours span midnight | Store in UTC, display in local |
| **Holiday calendars** | Availability varies by country | Configurable working days |
| **Overtime calculation** | Gap doesn't account for OT | Add "max overtime" parameter |
| **Skill mismatch** | Man-hours available вү  qualified | Add skill-based filtering |
| **Multi-depot planning** | Each depot has different capacity | Depot-level breakdown view |
| **Emergency maintenance** | Unplanned work disrupts schedule | Reserve buffer capacity |

---

## 13. Sources & References

- [Resource Histogram Best Practices - ProjectManager.com](https://www.projectmanager.com/blog/what-is-a-resource-histogram)
- [Capacity Planning Software - The Digital Project Manager](https://thedigitalprojectmanager.com/tools/best-capacity-planning-software/)
- [Choosing Chart Types - Nielsen Norman Group](https://www.nngroup.com/articles/choosing-chart-types/)
- [Data Visualization Handbook - UX Magazine](https://uxmag.com/articles/the-ultimate-data-visualization-handbook-for-designers)
- [Stacked Bar Charts - Domo](https://www.domo.com/learn/charts/stacked-bar-charts)
- [Workload Management Tools - Runn](https://www.runn.io/blog/workload-management-tools)

---

## 14. Approval Checklist

- [ ] Reviewed by UX Designer
- [ ] Reviewed by Product Owner
- [ ] RFQ Section 8 compliance verified
- [ ] Accessibility audit passed
- [ ] Performance benchmarks met
- [ ] Approved for implementation

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Storybook 8 and PatternFly React integration:

## Storybook 8 Overview

Storybook is the industry standard UI tool for building, testing, and documenting components and pages. It's used by thousands of teams globally, integrates with all major JavaScript frameworks, and combines with most leading design and developer tools.(no content)Storybook 8 brings major improvements including built-in visual testing, React Server Component support, upgraded Vue and React control autogeneration, rearchitected Vite support, Vitest testing, Vite 5 support, 2-4x faster test builds, a refreshed desktop UI, rebuilt mobile UX, and removed React dependency for non-React projects.

## Setting Up Storybook 8 with React

When initializing Storybook 8 for a React project with Vite, the installer adds Storybook version 8.4.7 with dev dependencies including `@storybook/addon-essentials`, `@storybook/addon-interactions`, `@storybook/addon-onboarding`, `@storybook/blocks`, `@storybook/react`, `@storybook/react-vite`, `@storybook/test`, and `eslint-plugin-storybook`.

## PatternFly React Integration

PatternFly React seed is an open source build scaffolding utility that gives developers a jump-start when creating new projects that will use PatternFly.(no content)For TypeScript projects, you can fork and clone the PatternFly React Seed project. For JavaScript, you can fork and clone the JavaScript branch of the PatternFly React Seed project.(no content)The patternfly-react project uses React Storybook to test and demo new PatternFly 3 React UI components.

## PatternFly Elements (Web Components) with React

PatternFly Elements ships with React wrappers for each web component, making working with web components within React JSX files more convenient.(no content)To set up PatternFly Elements with React, you need to install `@patternfly/elements` and the `@lit/react` library, then import the various components in your App.tsx file.(no content)React needs special treatment when integrating with web components due to its reliance on virtual DOM and JSX. PatternFly Elements uses the `@lit/react` library to create React wrapper components.

For more specific and up-to-date setup instructions, I recommend checking the official PatternFly React documentation on GitHub (https://github.com/patternfly/patternfly-react) and the Storybook documentation for React (https://storybook.js.org/tutorials/intro-to-storybook/react/en/get-started/).

Here's a summary of the search results for setting up Storybook with Playwright for visual regression testing:

## Overview

You can set up Playwright with Storybook to catch any regressions in UI components.(no content)Although Playwright is primarily known for its end-to-end testing capabilities, it also supports creating screenshots of UI components and comparing them with previous screenshots to detect any unforeseen changes to styling.

## Key Setup Approaches

### 1. **Direct Playwright Configuration**

The basic Playwright config requires a configurable base URL (defaulting to `http://localhost:6006`), using the `html` reporter for visual diffing, and running regression tests for at least desktop and mobile devices using projects like "Desktop Chrome" and "iPhone 15".

### 2. **Storybook Test Runner with Playwright**

Storybook includes a Playwright-based Test Runner to execute your stories as tests.(no content)Thousands of teams use this to functionally test their components, while others want to reuse their stories in other testing tools like Playwright CT (Component testing).

### 3. **Portable Stories API**

In your test file, you can import portable stories and use the `createTest` API from Storybook, which extends the base test API from Playwright. Upon mounting a story, all of its decorators, loaders, and play function will execute correctly.

## Best Practices for Storybook Stories

Component examples should be pure with no external styles or headersвҖ”just a pure representation of the component. Use one state per story. If your component has multiple states, represent them in their own stories to increase test coverage.(no content)Avoid dynamic content. Content that is randomized or animation-heavy makes visual testing harder. You'll have a much easier time if you keep things in a consistent state.

## Docker for Consistent Results

In order to get the same results across machines, you should run Playwright in a Docker container.(no content)Setting the `TEST_BASE_URL` to `http://host.docker.internal:6006` ensures the Docker container can reach Storybook running on your host machine.

## Handling Flakiness

Dialing in tests to reduce flakiness is just part of VRTвҖ”even paid VRT products struggle with this. Fortunately, you can set sensible defaults in your global config as well as using overrides in individual tests, and Playwright has pretty great defaults out-of-the-box.(no content)`maxDiffPixels` is great at catching precise regressions such as thin border changes but increases test flakiness by not dealing well with text antialiasing noise. `maxDiffPixelRatio` is ideal for text-heavy screenshots, but can sometimes be too loose when catching subtle pixel regressions for larger components.

## Alternative Tools

Other visual regression testing options include Playwright, Loki, Chromatic, Percy, and Lost Pixel.(no content)The team behind Storybook offers Chromatic for visual regression testing, but at 149 bucks a month, it's not cheap.

## CI Integration

Tests can run in GitHub Actions on every PR and report any failures. Playwright lists out specific tests that failed in its error logs, and you can download the generated artifact to see the actual diff.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Best practices for organizingвҖҰ | by Varun Vachhar | Storybook | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/storybookjs/structuring-your-storybook-5148b116b874"},{"title":"A Guide to Implementing Storybook in Your Enterprise Project (Storybook series part 2) | Divotion","url":"https://www.divotion.com/blog/storybook-in-your-enterprise-project"},{"title":"Naming components and hierarchy | Storybook docs","url":"https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/naming-components-and-hierarchy"},{"title":"Atomic design system implementation using Storybook","url":"https://blogs.halodoc.io/atomic-design-system-implementation-at-halodoc/"},{"title":"GitHub - danilowoz/react-atomic-design: рҹ”¬ How the Atomic Design methodology can create a great design system from scratch and make better developers.","url":"https://github.com/danilowoz/react-atomic-design"},{"title":"Atomic Design in React: Best Practices","url":"https://propelius.ai/blogs/atomic-design-in-react-best-practices"},{"title":"Build a simple component | Storybook Tutorials","url":"https://storybook.js.org/tutorials/intro-to-storybook/react/en/simple-component/"}]

Here are the search results on Storybook 8, Vite, and best practices for component organization using atomic design:

## Storybook with Vite & Atomic Design - Best Practices

### Setting Up Storybook with Vite

To create a new React project with Vite and TypeScript, you can use `npm create vite@latest my-storybook-app -- --template react-ts`, then navigate into the project directory and install dependencies.(no content)The Storybook init command automatically configures Storybook for your Vite setup. It creates a `.storybook/` folder containing configuration files and adds example stories in `src/stories/`.

### Atomic Design Methodology

Pioneered by Brad Frost, Atomic Design is a common hierarchical system for UIs. It classifies components into five levels: atoms, molecules, organisms, templates, and pages.(no content)Atoms are the smallest possible components, such as buttons, titles, inputs or even color palettes, animations, and fonts. They can be applied in any context, globally or within other components and templates, and have many states (like disabled, hover, different sizes, etc.).

### Component Organization Best Practices

When creating your stories, you can explicitly use the title parameter to define the story's position in the sidebar. It can also be used to group related components together in an expandable interface to help with Storybook organization.

For example, to organize using atomic design in Storybook:
```typescript
// Checkbox.stories.js
import { Checkbox } from './Checkbox';

export default { 
  title: 'Design System/Atoms/Checkbox', 
  component: Checkbox, 
};
```

By organizing your components into atoms, molecules, and organisms, you make it easier to communicate about your UI and reuse components across different parts of your application. Additionally, by structuring your Storybook stories in the same way, you can easily navigate and explore your UI components. Overall, using the atomic design approach can help improve your team's productivity, reduce bugs and inconsistencies, and make your UI more flexible and maintainable.

### Story Types and Documentation

Start with a documentation page explaining what the component does and when to use it. This is also an excellent place to include the design specs, visual guidelines, responsive behaviour, and accessibility do's and don'ts.(no content)Next is a Playground story, where all the component props are wired up to Storybook controls. This works well for demonstrating the component API and prototyping in the browser.

### Storybook Configuration with Vite

In your `.storybook/main.ts`, you can configure Storybook with the Vite builder using `core: { builder: "@storybook/builder-vite" }` along with essential addons like `@storybook/addon-links`, `@storybook/addon-essentials`, and `@storybook/addon-interactions`.

### Testing and Development Benefits

Storybook is a powerful tool for testing and developing atomic components in isolation. It allows you to focus on individual components, ensuring they work as expected before integrating them into the larger application. By creating stories that showcase various states and configurations of each component, you can quickly test their behavior under different conditions.(no content)Organizing these stories by feature or functionality with clear, descriptive names makes it easier for the team to navigate and reuse components. Storybook can also be extended with add-ons and integrated with testing tools like Jest or Cypress.

### Common Challenges

One of the trickiest parts of Atomic Design is figuring out where a component fits - should it be an atom, molecule, or organism? This classification process can eat up valuable time and even lead to disagreements within the team.(no content)Some teams group their components by status - whether a UI component is ready to be used, experimental, or deprecated. This strategy works particularly well for design system teams like IBM Carbon and Workday Canvas.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"Mock Service Worker | Storybook integrations","url":"https://storybook.js.org/addons/msw-storybook-addon"},{"title":"Mocking network requests | Storybook docs","url":"https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/mocking-data-and-modules/mocking-network-requests"},{"title":"Mock Service Worker В· GitHub","url":"https://github.com/mswjs"},{"title":"Introduction - Mock Service Worker","url":"https://mswjs.io/docs/"},{"title":"Using Storybook and Mock Service Worker for mocked API responses - LogRocket Blog","url":"https://blog.logrocket.com/using-storybook-and-mock-service-worker-for-mocked-api-responses/"},{"title":"GitHub - mswjs/msw-storybook-addon: Mock API requests in Storybook with Mock Service Worker.","url":"https://github.com/mswjs/msw-storybook-addon"},{"title":"Mocking API on Storybook using MSW - This Dot Labs","url":"https://www.thisdot.co/blog/mocking-api-on-storybook-using-msw"},{"title":"Mock Service Worker, Storybook, Jest, and React in 2024 by Suhan Wijaya","url":"https://www.suhanwijaya.com/posts/using-msw-with-storybook-jest-dev"},{"title":"How to Use Mock Service Worker (MSW) and Storybook for Efficient UI Development - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/samuel_kinuthia/how-to-use-mock-service-worker-msw-and-storybook-for-efficient-ui-development-637"},{"title":"Mock Service Worker Addon Panel | Storybook integrations","url":"https://storybook.js.org/addons/storybook-msw-addon"}]

Here are the search results for MSW Mock Service Worker Storybook fixtures mock data strategy 2025:

## Key Findings

### MSW Storybook Addon Setup

MSW is an API mocking library, which relies on service workers to capture network requests and provides mocked data in response. The MSW addon brings this functionality into Storybook, allowing you to mock API requests in your stories.

### Installation and Configuration

You can enable MSW in Storybook by initializing MSW and providing the MSW loader in `./storybook/preview.js`:

```javascript
import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'
initialize()

const preview = {
  parameters: { /* your other code... */ },
  loaders: [mswLoader],
}
export default preview
```

### Fixtures and Mock Data Strategy

MSW provides tools to model and query your mock data (fixtures).(no content)You can also generate request handlers from various sources like HAR files and OpenAPI documents.

### Request Handler Patterns

You can pass request handlers into the handlers property of the msw parameter. This is commonly an array of handlers:

```javascript
import { http, HttpResponse } from 'msw'

export const SuccessBehavior = {
  parameters: {
    msw: {
      handlers: [
        http.get('/user', () => {
          return HttpResponse.json({ firstName: 'Neil', lastName: 'Maverick' })
        }),
      ],
    },
  },
}
```

### Reusable Mock Strategy

Request handlers should be declared in a separate module because they can be reused for multiple purposes: within your Storybook, during local development, for testing, or for debugging. "Write once, reuse anywhere."(no content)It's recommended to keep the "success" paths of any API in the global mocks/handlers.js module, while delegating the per-scenario overrides (such as error responses) closer to each individual usage surface (i.e., a specific story, or an integration test).

### Standalone API Mocking Layer

By treating API mocking as a standalone layer, MSW can integrate throughout your entire stack, allowing you to reuse and customize network behavior on demand. You can use the same API mocks during development, integration and end-to-end testing.

### Testing Integration

For testing with portable stories, you can use the `applyRequestHandlers` helper before rendering your story, and use `composeStories` from `@storybook/react` to reuse your MSW handlers in tests.

### Benefits of This Approach

As your app grows, it will be much simpler to add new API helper modules, mocks, and MSW resolvers. This offers numerous benefits, including faster development cycles, more consistent testing, easier simulation of API behaviors, and perhaps a happier collaboration with your backend teams.

```

### Fixtures and Mock Data Strategy

MSW provides tools to model and query your mock data (fixtures).(no content)You can also generate request handlers from various sources like HAR files and OpenAPI documents.

### Request Handler Patterns

You can pass request handlers into the handlers property of the msw parameter. This is commonly an array of handlers:

```javascript
import { http, HttpResponse } from 'msw'

export const SuccessBehavior = {
  parameters: {
    msw: {
      handlers: [
        http.get('/user', () => {
          return HttpResponse.json({ firstName: 'Neil', lastName: 'Maverick' })
        }),
      ],
    },
  },
}
```

### Reusable Mock Strategy

Request handlers should be declared in a separate module because they can be reused for multiple purposes: within your Storybook, during local development, for testing, or for debugging. "Write once, reuse anywhere."(no content)It's recommended to keep the "success" paths of any API in the global mocks/handlers.js module, while delegating the per-scenario overrides (such as error responses) closer to each individual usage surface (i.e., a specific story, or an integration test).

### Standalone API Mocking Layer

By treating API mocking as a standalone layer, MSW can integrate throughout your entire stack, allowing you to reuse and customize network behavior on demand. You can use the same API mocks during development, integration and end-to-end testing.

### Testing Integration

For testing with portable stories, you can use the `applyRequestHandlers` helper before rendering your story, and use `composeStories` from `@storybook/react` to reuse your MSW handlers in tests.

### Benefits of This Approach

As your app grows, it will be much simpler to add new API helper modules, mocks, and MSW resolvers. This offers numerous benefits, including faster development cycles, more consistent testing, easier simulation of API behaviors, and perhaps a happier collaboration with your backend teams.

Here are the search results for PatternFly 5 dashboard, KPI card, and data table component examples:

## PatternFly Design System - Dashboard, Card, and Table Components

### Dashboard Pattern
PatternFly supports several common card layouts to help convey information in a consistent way. This is not an exhaustive list, but before designing your cards, you should see if the information you are trying to communicate fits into any of these common patterns. Note that card layouts are flexible and can be customized. If you don't see exactly what you are looking for here, we recommend that you use these standard layouts as a basis upon which to build your custom designs.

#### Aggregate Status Cards
Aggregate status cards show a total number of objects and an aggregated status for them. For example, they can be used to provide users with a quick count of the number of nodes making up a large distributed network and identify the numbers that are down, that need maintenance, etc. There would also typically be a link to examine more details.

#### Trend Cards (KPI Cards)
Use the title of the card to reflect the current value. Place a filter in the upper right corner of the card when you want to filter data in the chart. Most often this will be used to change the time range of the chart, e.g. "Last 24 hours," "Last 7 days," "Last 30 days," etc. Sparkline charts are most often used to display trend data.(no content)Regular trend cards arrange the current value and trend chart in a stacked layout. You can use the short trend card layout to save vertical space by placing the current value and the trend chart side-by-side. You may also want to monitor trends for several metrics on the same card. If you're displaying multiple trend metrics on a card, stack trend charts with clear naming to indicate what each chart measures.

### Table Component
The Table component takes an explicit and declarative approach, and its implementation closely mirrors that of an HTML table. This Table component allows you to compose the table by nesting the relevant Thead, Tbody, Tr, Th and Td components within it.(no content)Provide dataLabel prop to the Td components so that in mobile view the cell has a label to provide context. If you want a table caption, simply place a `<Caption>My caption</Caption>` as the first child within a Table.

### Card Component
A card is a content container that displays entry-level informationвҖ”typically within dashboards, galleries, and catalogs. When included, headers define the contents of a card. Card headers can contain images as well as the title of a card and an actions menu represented by the right-aligned kebab. In most cases, your card should include a header.

### Data List Component
PatternFly offers 2 components for displaying large data sets: data lists and tables. While they satisfy similar use cases, choosing the correct component to use in your design will be dependent on the type of data you need to display.

**When to use a Data List:**
The information you want to display is not easily structured into a tabular format. Content displayed varies between rows and you want a more flexible layout. You plan to embed rich content like a chart or an image into a row.

### Key Resources:
- **Dashboard Guidelines**: https://www.patternfly.org/patterns/dashboard/design-guidelines/
- **Table Component**: https://www.patternfly.org/components/table/
- **Card Component**: https://www.patternfly.org/components/card/design-guidelines/
- **Data List Component**: https://www.patternfly.org/components/data-list/design-guidelines/
- **GitHub Dashboard Demo**: https://github.com/patternfly/patternfly/blob/main/src/patternfly/demos/Dashboard/examples/Dashboard.md


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.
